evals.prompt: I want to build an MCP server that gives AI clients access to CoinGecko's free public API. Users should be able to: get current prices for any cryptocurrency by name or symbol, see the top 10 gainers and losers over the last 24 hours, and search for a coin by name. No API key needed — CoinGecko's public endpoints are free. I plan to deploy it as an HTTP server on Railway.
evals.expected_output: A complete TypeScript MCP project scaffolded with: client-compatible tool names (e.g. crypto_price, crypto_gainers, crypto_search unless the target clients are verified to support dotted names), server instructions field, all tools annotated with READONLY_EXTERNAL (since they call an external API), parameter .describe() + .meta({ title }) on every input, getOrFetch caching with appropriate TTL, safeFetch with error handling, smithery.remote-config.json (since it's hosted), package.json with all metadata fields including homepage and repository, hosted discovery surfaces for AI search, and a smithery.remote-config.json file.
expectations: Tool names are specific and client-compatible (e.g. crypto_price or coin_price, not vague get_price or getCryptoPrice)
All tools have READONLY_EXTERNAL annotations (readOnlyHint: true and openWorldHint: true)
All parameters have .describe() with example values
All parameters have .meta({ title }) set
Server has an instructions field explaining tool usage order
Live API calls are wrapped in getOrFetch() with a TTL constant inside the handler body (not just imported)
All tool handlers have try/catch that returns isError: true on error
package.json includes homepage, repository, keywords, description, author, license — none are placeholder values like 'https://github.com/user/repo'
smithery.remote-config.json is present (since it's a hosted HTTP server)
Hosted discovery surfaces are planned or generated: /health, /llms.txt, sitemap, and crawlable text/JSON pages for AI search
Docs clearly state that ChatGPT Search can cite pages but cannot execute MCP tools unless connected as an app/connector
At least one prompt is registered using server.registerPrompt()
Every tool description starts with a verb (Get, List, Search, Find, Create) — not 'This tool', 'A tool', or a noun phrase
The registered prompt's message text explicitly names at least one tool by its actual client-compatible name (e.g. 'Call crypto_price' or 'use crypto_search') — not a generic template

evals.prompt: I have an existing MCP server I need you to audit and fix. The source file is at evals/files/broken-mcp-index.ts. It's a recipe finder MCP. Please audit it, fix all the issues, and tell me the estimated Smithery score before and after.
evals.expected_output: The audit identifies: noun-first descriptions, missing annotations, missing parameter .describe() and .meta(), no server instructions, static data loaded inside handlers (not at startup), no error handling (throws instead of returning isError:true), no caching, no prompts, vague get_ prefix naming, and missing discovery guidance. All issues are fixed in the output code. A before/after score report is provided. package.json and smithery config files are written as output files.
expectations: Identifies and fixes vague get_* tool naming to specific client-compatible names (recipe_list, recipe_detail, recipe_search or similar)
Identifies and fixes the missing annotations (adds readOnlyHint: true)
Identifies and fixes the missing .describe() on parameters (cuisine, maxTime, id, ingredients, matchAll)
Identifies and fixes the static file loading inside handlers (moves readFileSync to module level)
Identifies and fixes the missing try/catch in get_recipe_detail that throws instead of returning isError
Adds server instructions field to McpServer options
Provides a before/after estimated Smithery score
Mentions the 20pt Smithery UI manual steps (icon, display name, server description)
Writes package.json as an output file with name, description, homepage, repository, keywords — not just suggests adding these fields
Writes smithery.yaml or smithery.remote-config.json as an output file with required: []
Does not force dotted tool names if target clients are Claude clients that may not support them reliably

evals.prompt: Build me an MCP that exposes OpenWeatherMap data. I have an API key. I want three tools: get current weather for a city, get a 5-day forecast for a city, and compare current weather across multiple cities. This will run locally via npx on Claude Desktop.
evals.expected_output: A complete TypeScript MCP with: client-compatible tool names (weather_current, weather_forecast, weather_compare or similar), smithery.yaml with configSchema that has apiKey as optional (required: []), the API key passed via environment variable, READONLY_EXTERNAL annotations (external API), appropriate TTL caching (30min for weather, 4h for forecast), parameter .describe() + .meta(), server instructions, package.json with all fields, MCPB packaging guidance for one-click local setup, and a prompt for a typical weather workflow.
expectations: Tool names are client-compatible (e.g. weather_current, weather_forecast, weather_compare)
smithery.yaml is generated with configSchema block (not smithery.remote-config.json — this is a stdio server)
smithery.yaml has required: [] so the API key is optional not required
API key is accessed via process.env not hardcoded
Weather tools use READONLY_EXTERNAL annotation (openWorldHint: true)
weather_current uses a shorter TTL constant (30min range) than weather_forecast (4h range) — verified in the handler call, not just the TTL definition
All parameters have .describe() with concrete example values
Server instructions mention which tool to call first and in what order
package.json has homepage, repository, keywords, description — none are the boilerplate placeholder 'https://github.com/user/repo'
Every tool description starts with a verb (Get, List, Search, Compare) — not 'This tool' or a noun phrase
README or release checklist includes MCPB packaging guidance for reducing Claude Desktop setup friction
The registered prompt message text explicitly names at least one of the three weather tools by name

If the server startup validates API key presence (e.g. checks for empty key), the smithery.yaml commandFunction always passes the env var — or the server handles a missing key gracefully without calling process.exit(1)

evals.prompt: I have an existing MCP server I need you to audit and fix. The source file is at evals/files/broken-mcp-index.ts. It's a recipe finder MCP. Please audit it, fix all the issues including any resource issues, and tell me the estimated Smithery score before and after.
evals.expected_output: The audit maps registered resources in addition to tools and identifies the `cuisines` resource, which returns JSON content but declares no mimeType in either the registration metadata or the read callback's contents entry. The fix adds mimeType: "application/json" in both places.
expectations: Audit builds a resources table (name, URI scheme, mimeType declared?) in addition to the tools table
Identifies the cuisines resource as missing an explicit mimeType for its JSON content
Fix adds mimeType: "application/json" to the resource registration metadata (third argument to registerResource)
Fix adds mimeType: "application/json" to the contents[] entry returned by the read callback
Does not misidentify the cuisines resource as a tool, and does not suggest removing it instead of fixing the mimeType

evals.prompt: I have an existing MCP server I need you to audit and fix. The source file is at evals/files/broken-mcp-index.ts. It's a recipe finder MCP. Please audit it, fix all the issues including any resource template and completions issues, and tell me the estimated Smithery score before and after.
evals.expected_output: The audit enumerates resource templates separately from static resources (resources/templates/list) and identifies two issues: the cuisine-recipes template's read callback destructures `cuisine` while the URI template variable is `cuisineSlug`, and the ingredient-info template's `complete` map key `ingredient` doesn't match its URI template variable `ingredientName`, so the completions capability never gets declared even though the callback exists. Both are fixed: the read callback is corrected to destructure `cuisineSlug`, and the complete map key is corrected to `ingredientName`.
expectations: Audit builds a resource templates table separate from the static resources table, noting param/URI variable name match and completion registration
Identifies the cuisine-recipes template's read callback destructuring `cuisine` when the URI template variable is `cuisineSlug`
Fix renames the destructured parameter to `cuisineSlug` (or renames the URI template variable to `cuisine`, but not both diverging)
Identifies the ingredient-info template's complete map key `ingredient` as not matching the URI template variable `ingredientName`
Fix renames the complete map key to `ingredientName` so the callback is reachable and the completions capability gets declared
Does not conflate the two bugs: the cuisine-recipes fix does not touch the complete map, and the ingredient-info fix does not touch the read callback destructuring
Confirms (or notes as verification) that after the fix, completions appears in the server's declared capabilities
